ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image and (B) size distribution (%) of carbon quantum dot (CQD) synthesized from spent coffee ground (SCG).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of carbon quantum dot (CQD) synthesized from spent coffee ground (SCG): (A) Survey spectrum, (B) High resolution C1s spectra, and (C) High resolution N1s spectra.

A linear plot of integral photoluminescence intensity (excitation: 360 nm; emission: 450 nm) for the absorption at 360 nm of carbon quantum dot (CQD) synthesized from spent coffee ground (SCG) at pH (A) 2.5, (B) 3.0, and (C) 4.0 obtained from three independent trials. Fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) value was derived by comparing the derived slope with the reference value of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (54 %). The same capital letter indicates no significant (P > 0.05) difference between values.

Time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) decay measurements of carbon quantum dot (CQD) synthesized from spent coffee ground (SCG) at pH (A) 2.5, (B) 3.0, and (C) 4.0 with excitation and emission wavelengths at 375 nm and 450 nm, respectively. Average fluorescence lifetime (τave) was calculated using the following formula: (a 1τ1 2+a 2τ2 2+a 3τ3 2)/(a 1τ1+a 2τ2+a 3τ3), where a 1, a 2, and a 3 are amplitudes as a normalized percentage (%) of the first, second, and third components, respectively, and τ1, τ2, and τ3 are the PL lifetime (ns) of these components, respectively. The same capital letter indicates no significant (P > 0.05) difference between values.

(A) Zeta potentials (mV) of carbon quantum dot (CQD) synthesized from spent coffee ground (SCG), Escherichia coli O157:H7, and Staphylococcus aureus at different pH condition (2.5, 3.0, and 4.0). Each point and error bar indicate the mean value from three replications and standard deviation, respectively.

Uptake values of carbon quantum dot (CQD) synthesized from spent coffee ground (SCG) into cells of Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli O157:H7 at different pH conditions (2.5, 3.5, and 4.0). Data indicate an average of three independent experiments and standard deviations are shown by error bars. The same capital letter within same pathogen indicates no significant (P > 0.05) difference.

Relative activities of (A and C) superoxide dismutase (SOD) and (B and D) catalase (CAT) of (A and B) Escherichia coli O157:H7 and (C and D) Staphylococcus aureus at different pH conditions (2.5, 3.0, 4.0, and 7.0). Each point and error bar indicate the mean value and standard deviation from three replications, respectively.

Intracellular reactive oxygen species (iROS) generation in Staphylococcus aureus incubated with PBS in the presence or absence of 1 mg/mL of carbon quantum dot (CQD) synthesized from spent coffee ground (SCG) at different pH conditions (2.5, 3.0, and 4.0) under visible light treatment of 84.24 J/cm2. Data indicate an average of three independent experiments and standard deviations are shown by error bars. Different capital letters within the same CQD inclusion condition (with or without) indicate significant (P < 0.05) difference. The same lowercase letter under the same pH condition indicates no significant (P > 0.05) difference.

Degrees of (A) destruction and (B) lipid peroxidation in cell membrane of Staphylococcus aureus in PBS with or without 1 mg/mL of carbon quantum dot (CQD) synthesized from spent coffee ground (SCG) at different pH conditions (2.5, 3.0, and 4.0) under visible light treatment of 84.24 J/cm2. Data indicate an average of three independent experiments and standard deviations are shown by error bars. Different capital letters under the same CQD inclusion condition (with or without) indicates significant (P < 0.05) difference. The same lowercase letter under the same pH condition indicates no significant (P > 0.05) difference.
Graphical explanation of the mechanism by which the photocatalytic bactericidal activity of carbon quantum dots synthesized from spent coffee ground pH presented in this study.

Surviving levels (log CFU/cm2 or log CFU/5 ml) of Escherichia coli O157:H7 on apple surface or in a washing solution [distilled water (DW)] after combined treatment with carbon quantum dot (1 mg/ml) derived from spent coffee ground and (A) 0, (B) 1.0, (C) 1.5, (D) 2.0 % malic acid under conditions with or without visible light irradiation. Data indicate an average of three independent experiments and standard deviations are shown by error bars.
Applications of carbon quantum dots synthesized from spent coffee grounds (SCGs).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dot synthesized using spent coffee ground as a biomass exhibiting visible-light-driven antimicrobial activity against foodborne pathogens

ce:section.ce:para: Globally, cross-contamination from minimally processed products such as fresh produce is considered a major risk factor for foodborne outbreaks (Yi et al., 2020). Cross-contamination of minimally processed products during post-harvest processing such as washing treatment is usually caused by transfer from pathogens of contaminated produce to uncontaminated produce in the washing water (Ding and Tikekar, 2020). Therefore, it is important to reduce the risk of cross-contamination by controlling foodborne pathogens in the washing water to suppress their spread. However, conventional water disinfection methods such as chemical sanitizing agents and ultraviolet (UV) treatment have unavoidable limitations such as formation of harmful disinfection byproduct, not being eco-friendly, and cost problems such as energy consumption (Zhang et al., 2019). In this regard, there is a need to explore next-generation water disinfectant technology that is safe, high efficient, cost-effective, eco-friendly, and sustainable.

Semiconductor photocatalytic disinfection technology that utilizes visible light of natural light such as sunlight and indoor light is spotlighted as a promising technology because it is energy efficient and reusable without forming harmful by-products (Shi et al., 2021; C. Zhang et al., 2018a,b). In this technology, electron (e −)-hole (h +) pairs created on the surface of a semiconductor material by light energy can convert water and oxygen into reactive oxygen species (ROS) under ambient condition, thereby exerting a control effect on microorganisms (Tzeng et al., 2021). Because ROS are highly reactive that can non-specifically damage essential cellular components, they have a strong control effect on a wide range of microorganisms without inducing resistance (Tavares et al., 2010; Xia et al., 2015). Especially, nanostructured photocatalysts (NPCs) have demonstrated heightened activity due to their expanded surface area and aspect ratio, providing advantages such as enhanced light absorption, efficient charge separation, and increased photostability (Duan et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2017; You et al., 2019). Up to date, various forms of nanostructured photocatalysts such as TiO2, bismuth, carbon, MoS2, and ZnO based photocatalysts have been investigated as visible-light-driven (VLD) water disinfection technologies, and their potential has been demonstrated (You et al., 2019). Among them, carbon quantum dot (CQD), a new class of carbon-based nanoparticles with outstanding properties such as high stability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high water solubility, and low manufacturing cost, has a high potential for use in photocatalytic technology (Abd Rani, Ng, Ng and Mahmoudi, 2020; Wang et al., 2018). In particular, while conventional nano-sized semiconductor photosensitizers require UV light to be active, CQD can be activated in the visible light spectrum to exhibit antimicrobial activities against a wide range of microorganisms such as bacteria, antibiotic-resistant pathogens, and viruses (Dong et al., 2018, 2021; Dong et al., 2020).

Worldwide, about one-third of food produced for human consumption, which is equivalent to 1.3 billion tons per year, is lost or wasted. Such food waste occurs at all stages of the food supply chain ranging from production to consumption (Ebikade et al., 2021; Vilariño et al., 2017). Most of such waste food is directly thrown away, landfilled, or burned, which can cause serious environmental problems and economic loss due to additional treatment or waste of resources (Babbar and Oberoi, 2014; Kang et al., 2020a,b). Based on these facts, devising a sustainable method through an upcycling strategy that can convert food waste into useful materials is urgently needed. It has been demonstrated that CQD can be synthesized from any starting materials containing carbon. Food waste is a natural organic carbon source, meaning that it can be utilized for synthesizing CQD (Kang et al., 2020a,b; Yi et al., 2020). Utilizing food waste as a synthetic material for CQD not only helps us solve inherent problems of food waste, but also possesses advantages of using a very good synthetic material because food waste is sustainable, eco-friendly, inexpensive, easy to obtain, and abundant as a carbon source (Meng et al., 2019; Shahraki et al., 2022).

It is known that various properties of CQD can be changed by doping, through which elements other than carbon can be additionally introduced into CQD to change the carbon framework or surface chemical structure (Kou et al., 2020; Miao et al., 2020). Food waste is a material that has abundant proteins. Nitrogen and sulfur also exist in large proportions in amino acids of proteins in addition to carbon (Prandi et al., 2019). Thus, when synthesizing CQD using food waste, N and S can be doped. However, since compositions and contents of amino acids vary by food type (Gardner et al., 2019), CQD with different characteristics will be synthesized depending on the type of food by-products. In a previous study, we have investigated VLD antimicrobial activity of CQD according to nitrogen and/or sulfur doping in amino acids and demonstrated that the activity of CQD is increased when the content of amino acid is increased while the ratio of amino acid containing sulfur is decreased (Kang and Kang, 2021). About 50% of spent coffee ground (SCG), a by-product discarded after coffee extraction, is in the form of (hemi)-cellulose, which has abundant carbon. During the extraction process, only about 33% of proteins are extracted. The rest remains as insoluble residues due to denaturation and so on. Thus, SCG has a high amino acid content (Campos-Vega et al., 2015). According to a previous study (Martinez-Saez et al., 2017), proportions of cysteine and methionine as amino acids containing S are very low (about 0.4%) in SCG. Thus, CQD synthesized using SCG is expected to show excellent VLD antimicrobial activity. Furthermore, it is known that coffee beans have similar protein contents even if coffee species and roasting methods are different, meaning that CQD with relatively constant characteristics can be synthesized using SCG (Casal et al., 2005; Cruz et al., 2012; Martinez-Saez et al., 2017). For these reasons, SCG is considered a suitable raw material for synthesizing CQD with VLD antimicrobial activity.

According to reports from the International Coffee Organization, economic trade involving coffee has shown consistent growth over time, with further expansion anticipated in the coffee industry (Leow et al., 2021). Remarkably, coffee holds the distinction of being the second-largest globally traded commodity, following petroleum. This prominence in trade is attributed to the substantial output of by-products from coffee production and processing, which encompasses elements such as coffee pulp, husks, silver skin, and SCG (Murthy and Naidu, 2012; Mussatto et al., 2011). Notably, from every ton of green coffee, a substantial 650 kg of SCGs can be derived (Mussatto et al., 2011). Clearly, SCGs are notable by-products produced during the process of making instant coffee. They are commonly disposed of due to their minimal economic value (Leow et al., 2021). As a result, researchers worldwide are actively exploring various applications for spent coffee grounds and their products, including biofuels, catalysts, cosmetics, composite materials, and ingredients for both animal feed and food (Franca and Oliveira, 2022). In this context, several researchers have synthesized CQDs using SCGs and applying them to various fields, suggesting the possibility of utilizing SCG in a sustainable form through upcycling into nanomaterials (Table 1 ). To extend these studies and suggest the possibility of using CQDs synthesized from SCGs in new fields, this study will examine the value of using it as a VLD photocatalyst for water disinfection, which has not been reported yet.

In this study, CQDs were synthesized using SCGs. The possibility of applying CODs as visible light-driven catalytic reaction-based water disinfectants was verified. For this purpose, antimicrobial effects of SCGs-derived CQD on Staphylococcus aureus (Gram-positive bacteria) and Escherichia coli O157:H7 (Gram-negative bacteria) under visible light irradiation were investigated. It is known that CQD can change their properties such as fluorescence intensity (Song et al., 2016; Lei et al., 2019), bacterial growth inhibition activity (P. P. Li et al., 2020), and catalytic activity (Tripathi et al., 2020) depending on the pH condition. Thus, we evaluated VLD antimicrobial activities according to pH conditions. The mechanism involved in the change of activity by pH was further investigated.

SCG (Arabica) was used as a source of carbon for the synthesis of CQD. It was bought from a local cafeteria in Seoul, South Korea. SCG (5 g) was added into 30 mL of ultrapure distilled water in a 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask and incubated at 70 °C in water bath for 10 min. The completely dissolved solution was placed in a domestic microwave (700 W) for 5 min at the maximum power. It was then allowed to cool down to room temperature (22 ± 2 °C). Then 100 mL of ultrapure distilled water was added to the reacted solid product. After incubating at 70 °C in a water bath for 30 min, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 g for 20 min to obtain a supernatant. The supernatant was filtered with a syringe filter (0.22 μm). After that, a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500–1000 Da) was rinsed in ultrapure distilled water and used to dialyze the filtered solution. The dialysis membrane was soaked against ultrapure distilled water for 3 d, during which the ultrapure distilled water was changed every 24 h. The dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain dried CQD.

The synthesized CQD from SCG was characterized using various measuring instruments. For transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis, a CQD solution was prepared by dissolving CQD powder in ultrapure distilled water at a concentration of 0.01 mg/ml. The CQD solution was deposited on 400-mesh carbon-coated Cu grids. After the solvent was evaporated at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C), morphologies of particles were observed with a 200 kV TEM (JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan). During observations, similar morphologies were confirmed in various other areas, and one representative image was randomly selected.

Particle size distribution of CQD was analyzed through dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, UK) equipped with a He–Ne laser beam at λ = 633 nm. A CQD solution was prepared by dissolving CQD powder in ultrapure distilled water at a concentration of 0.01 mg/ml. Then polystyrene disposable cuvettes containing the CQD solution were exposed to a red light laser (wavelength, λ = 633 nm). All measurements took place after equilibration for 2 min at a temperature of 25 °C, utilizing backscatter detection at a scattering angle of 173°. Each outcome was the mean value derived from three subdivisions of measurements, each comprising a total of 100 individual measurements to ensure consistency in data acquisition. For XPS analysis, a CQD solution was prepared by dissolving CQD powder in ultrapure distilled water at a concentration of 1 mg/ml. The CQD solution was then drop cast onto a Si wafer and dried to form a thin film in a vacuum chamber. Analysis was conducted using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK) equipped with a microfocused Al Kɑ X-ray source operating at 1486.68 eV. During XPS analysis, spectra were acquired in a constant analyzer energy mode with a pass energy set to 200 eV. For specific narrow spectral regions, a pass energy of 50 eV was utilized. To counteract charging effects, an argon flood gun system was employed. Data acquisition and processing were performed using Thermo Scientific Avantage software. Surface surface compositions, presented in atomic percentages, were determined by analyzing integrated peak areas of detected elements along with their corresponding sensitivity factors. Photoluminescence (PL) lifetime of CQD was determined using a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system (PicoHarp300, PicoQuant GmbH, Germany). CQD solution was prepared by dissolving CQD powder in ultrapure distilled water at a concentration of 0.1 mg/ml. Sample solutions were excited using a 375 nm pulse (LDH-P-C-375, Picoquant), and the time-resolved fluorescence signals emitted by sample solutions were gathered at 450 nm. Exponential fitting for PL decays was achieved using a Symphotime-64 software (Ver. 2.2). PL lifetime (ns) was calculated with the following equation:
(a 1 τ 1 2 + a 2 τ 2 2 + a 3 τ 3 2) / (a 1 τ 1 + a 2 τ 2 + a 3 τ 3)
where a 1, a 2, and a 3 are amplitudes as a normalized percentage (%) of the first, second, and third components, respectively, and τ 1, τ 2, and τ 3 are the PL lifetime (ns) of these components, respectively.

Three strains (ATCC 43890, 43889, and 35150) of E. coli O157:H7 and three strains (ATCC 27213, 27649, and 25923) of S. aureus were obtained from the Food Safety Engineering Laboratory of Seoul National University (Seoul, Republic of Korea) for this study. Stock cultures stored at −80 °C were streaked onto tryptic soy agar (TSA; Difco, BD) plates, incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C as working culture. Prepared strains were cultured individually in 5 mL tryptic soy broth (Difco, BD, Sparks, MD) at 37 °C for 24 h. Cultured cells were collected by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 20 min. The pellet was washed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and re-suspended with PBS. Resuspended solutions of all pathogens were mixed together to form a culture cocktail comprised of approximately 109–1010 CFU/mL of each pathogen. This cultured cocktail was used in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial effect of CQD under visible light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a xenon lamp (100 W) equipped with a solar simulator (DXFL100, DTX Inc., Siheung, South Korea) and a UV cut-off lens (λ < 400) was used to provide visible light. After CQD at a concentration of 1 mg/mL was added to PBS, pH values were adjusted by adding hydrochloric acid (HCl, 0.1 M). Then, 5.0 mL of each solution was placed in a Petri dish (35 mm × 15 mm). After, 0.1 mL of inoculum was added to each solution, lid was closed. Since the samples prepared in this manner were used for each treatment condition, samples were prepared corresponding to the number of treatments conditions including the control. The vertical distance between the Petri dish and the lamp was 5 cm. The integral light intensity was measured with a fiber optic spectrometer (AvaSpec-ULS2048; Avantes, Eerbeek, Netherlands) at 400–800 nm for the sample surface. The integral light intensity was 7.8 mW/cm2. Doses of visible light used to treat samples were 0–84.24 J/cm2 (0–180 min) at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C). During treatment, samples were mixed via pipetting at 10 min intervals. After each treatment, 1 ml of each treated sample was transferred to 9 ml of 0.2% peptone water (Difco) and serially diluted ten-fold. Then 0.1 ml aliquots of dilutions were spread onto Sorbitol MacConkey Agar (SMAC, Difco) and Baird Parker Agar (MB cell, Kisan Biotech Co., Ltd., Seoul, Korea) plates for enumeration of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus, respectively. All plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Colonies on agar plate were enumerated and calculated as log10 CFU/mL. Using the same procedure, enumeration was conducted for a dark control which included CQD without visible light treatment and a light control which had only visible light treatment without CQD.

To measure fluorescence quantum yields, an established comparative method using known fluorescence standard sample was applied. Absorbance values of the sample and the standard at the same excitation wavelength were matched. Quantum yield values of samples were then obtained based on quantum yield values of the known standard. In this study, quinine sulfate (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 and used as the reference standard (FLQY: 54%). PL spectra of CQD dissolved in PBS with pH adjusted to 2.5, 3.0, and 4.0 using HCl and quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm in 10 mm path length fluorescence cuvettes. Integrated PL intensities were derived from PL spectra. Absorbance values of each CQD and reference standard samples were maintained below 0.1 to reduce the effect of re-absorption. A plot of integrated PL intensity and its corresponding absorbance value was obtained. The FLQY (%) was calculated with the following equations:
FLQYx(%) = FLQYs(Gradx/Grads)(ηs/ηx)
where subscripts s and x denoted the standard (quinine sulfate) and test (CQD with different pH) respectively, Grad was the slope in the equation, η was the refractive index of the solvent, and ηx and ηs were both 1.33.

Tauc modeling was applied to determine the band gap energy (Eg) of CQD from SCG. Tauc modeling equation was as follows:
α h ν
= A (
h ν
−
E g
)
n
where A was a constant, α was the absorption coefficient, hν was the photon energy, and n = 1/2 and 2 were for direct and indirect allowed transitions, respectively (Tyutrin et al., 2022; Y. Yang et al., 2017).

Considering CQD was a direct allowed type of transition semiconductor, the (αhv)2 versus hv plot (Tauc plot) was obtained using UV–vis absorption spectrum of C-dots. The band gap energy was determined by calculating the intercept of the incident photon energy axis by adding a straight part of the plot (Wang et al., 2015).

Surface charges of bacterial cells and CQD from SCG were evaluated by measuring zeta potentials of microorganisms and CQD at different pH values (2.5, 3.0, and 4.0). Bacterial cell suspensions were centrifuged at 4000 g for 20 min. Collected cell pellets were re-suspended in 10 mL of PBS. Pathogen suspension was 10-fold diluted in sterile PBS buffer and its pH was adjusted with 0.1 M HCl. CQD solution was prepared at a concentration of 1 mg/mL by adding 0.1 M HCl to adjust the pH. Zeta potential measurement was conducted with a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, UK).

To evaluate the degree of CQD uptake by bacterial cells depending on pH conditions, cells of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus were incubated with PBS containing 1 mg/ml CQD adjusted to pH 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, respectively, at an optical density of 1.5 at 600 nm for 3 h at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C). After incubation, bacterial cells were centrifuged at 10,000 g for 10 min and washed twice with PBS to remove CQD that was not uptaken. Final pelleted cells were re-suspended with PBS. Fluorescent intensities of cell suspension were measured with a spectrofluorophotometer (Spectramax M2e; Molecular Devices, Sunnyvale, CA, USA) at excitation/emission wavelengths of 360 nm/450 nm. Cell suspension not incubated with CQD solution was used as control.

Activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT) as ROS scavenging enzymes targeting O2 − and H2O2, respectively, were then measured. Briefly, E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus cells were incubated at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C) with PBS adjusted to pH 2.5, 3.0, and 4.0 at an optical density of 1.5 at 600 nm for 3 h. After incubation, cells were centrifuged at 10,000 g for 10 min and washed twice with PBS. Final pelleted cells were re-suspended with PBS. Re-suspended cells were disrupted with a sonicator (10 s on and 10 s off, six times) in an ice bath and centrifuged at 10,000 g for 10 min at 4 °C to collect scavenging enzymes remaining in the supernatant. SOD and CAT activities (%) were measured using an SOD assay kit -WST (Sigma-Aldrich) and an Amplex Red CAT assay kit (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA, USA), respectively, following each manufacturer's instructions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of intracellular ROS (iROS) generation and cell membrane damage
ce:section.ce:para: To measure the degree of iROS generation, structural damage, and lipid peroxidation, cellular dye 5-(and-6)-chloromethyl-2′,7' -dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (CM-H2DCFDA; Molecular Probes, Thermo Fisher Scientific), propidium iodide (PI; Sigma Aldrich), and diphenyl-1-pyrenylphosphine (DPPP; Sigma-Aldrich) were used. For the experiment, cells of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus cells with an optical density of 0.3 at 600 nm in PBS adjusted to pH 2.5, 3.0, and 4.0 with or without 1 mg/ml of CQD were treated with visible light and centrifuged at 10,000×g for 10 min followed by washing twice with PBS. Final cell pellets were incubated with CM-H2DCFDA, PI, or DPPP dye at a concentration of 5.0, 2.9, or 50 μM, respectively, for 10 min. After incubation, cells were harvested by centrifugation at 10,000×g for 10 min followed by washing twice with PBS. Fluorescence was measured with a spectrophotometer at excitation/emission wavelengths of 495/520, 493/630, and 351/380 nm for iROS generation, PI, DPPP assay, respectively. Using obtained florescent signals, each value was determined as follows: [(Fluorescence value of visible light treated cells) – (Fluorescence value of dark-treated cells).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQD combined with malic acid to fresh produce washing
ce:section.ce:para: Apples were purchased from a commercial local market (Seoul, South Korea). The apples were cut into 1 × 2 cm2, washed with DW, and dried for 30 min. Resuspended solution of E. coli O157:H7 prepared as described in section 2.3 was used as an inoculum. This inoculum was inoculated onto the surface of the sample and dried for 30 min to allow microbes to attach. Treatment solution was prepared by dissolving malic acid (MA; 99%, Samchun Pure Chemical Co., Ltd.) in CQD solution (1 mg/ml in DW) to a concentration of 0, 1.0, 1.5, or 2.0% (w/v), respectively. Then 5.0 mL of each treatment solution was placed in a Petri dish (35 mm × 15 mm). Each treatment solution prepared in this manner received one inoculated sample for treatment, so petri dishes with treatment solutions were prepared corresponding to the number of treatments conditions. Inoculated sample was put into the treatment solution and treated with visible light in the same experimental setup as described in section 2.4 while mixing at 200 rpm using a magnetic stirrer. Doses of visible light used to treat samples with were 0–14.04 J/cm2 (0–30 min) at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C). After treatment, each apple sample was transferred to a filter bag containing 100 ml of Dey/Engley (DE) neutralizing broth and homogenized. At the same time, 1 mL of the treatment solution was taken and transferred to 9 ml of DE neutralizing broth and vortexed. Each homogenized solution or solution diluted in DE broth was subjected to sequential 10-fold dilution to enumerate survival cells as described in section 2.4. Additionally, the same procedure was performed without visible light treatment (Dark treatment).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quality analysis of apple after washing treatment CQD combined with malic acid
ce:section.ce:para: To measure quality changes of apple samples after washing treatment using CQD combined with malic acid, color and texture changes were observed. Color changes of apple surfaces were assessed by employing a Minolta colorimeter (model CR400; Minolta Co., Osaka, Japan) at three randomly selected locations. Color values were measured for the same sample both before and after treatment. Average results of L*, a*, and b* values are then presented. Specifically, L* represents color lightness, a* indicates redness, and b* signifies yellowness of the sample.

The texture of each apple sample was assessed through texture profile analysis (TPA). A TA-XT2i texture analyzer (Stable Microsystems Ltd. in Surrey, England) equipped with an aluminum cylindrical probe with a diameter of 20 mm was utilized for conducting the texture tests. Operational settings included a pre-test speed of 2.00 mm/s, a test speed of 1.00 mm/s, a post-test speed of 2.00 mm/s, and a compression strain of 50%. The time interval between measurements was set at 5 s, and a trigger force of 0.05 N was applied. To evaluate changes in texture, hardness was selected as the key parameter. Hardness was quantified by identifying the peak deformation value on the curve. Five individual samples underwent measurement, and subsequently, the average hardness values were derived based on these five readings.

Except for TEM and XPS, all data were obtained through three independent replicate experiments and presented as mean and standard deviation. Data were subjected to analysis of variance (ANOVA) and the least significant difference (LSD) t-test using Statistical Analysis System (SAS) Version 9.4 (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC, USA). A probability level of P < 0.05 was used to determine significant differences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQD synthesized from SCG
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows morphology and particle size distribution measured with HRTEM imaging and DSL techniques of CQD, respectively. HRTEM (Fig. 1A) image showed small, uniform, and spherical shape structure of CQD with a lattice fringes of 0.290 nm, which might ascribe to the sp2 graphitic with crystalline structure (Lu et al., 2019). Fig. 1B shows size distribution of CQD synthesized from SCG with a narrow size distribution ranging from 0.6 to 22 nm. The average diameter of CQD was 11.2 nm. A previous study (Alam et al., 2015) has synthesized CQD from cabbage using a hydrothermal reactor with size ranging from 2 to 6 nm which is relatively smaller than the CQD synthesized from SCG. Prasannan and Imae (2013) have performed hydrothermal carbonization, resulting in a size distribution of 2–7 nm. However, CQDs from onions with autoclave and soy milk from hydrothermal have size distributions in the ranges of 7–25 nm and 13–40 nm, respectively (Bandi et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2012), similar to results of the present study. It could be interpreted that the size of CQD is various depending on raw materials and extraction methods.

To determine surface state and CQD content, XPS analysis was conducted (Fig. 2 ). Through XPS analysis, surface chemistry of CQD could be confirmed (Jansen and Van Bekkum, 1995). CQD from SCG contained C1s and N1s peaks. The atomic ratio of C1s: N1s: S2p elements was 88.68 : 11.10: 0.22. High-resolution C1s spectra revealed that CQD from SCG had peaks at 284.46, 285.83, 287.58, 288.43, and 292.42 eV related to C = C, C–N, C–O, C–C, and C–O–H, respectively (Ameen et al., 1993; Jansen and Van Bekkum, 1995; Liu et al., 2017a; Liu et al., 2017b; Oakley and Klobukowski, 2018). High resolution spectra of N1s showed peaks at 399.07, 399.69, 398.49 and 400.55 eV corresponding to –N = , C–N, pyridinic N, and C–N–H, respectively (Chen et al., 2021; Kavaklı et al., 2016; Y. Li et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020). These results revealed that CQD from SCG contained mainly C, O, and N. Alam et al. (2015) have confirmed that nitrogen doped on the surface of CQD can act as a passivating agent with oxygen. In particular, our previous study has demonstrated that N doping can increase the VLD antimicrobial activity of CQD (Kang and Kang, 2021). This means that CQD synthesized from SCG has suitable properties for water disinfectant using VLD catalytic reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of VLD antimicrobial activities of CQD synthesized from SCG at different pH conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of CQD synthesized from SCG as a VLD catalytic reaction-based water disinfectant, we investigated its antimicrobial activities against Gram-negative (E. coli O157:H7) and Gram-positive (S. aureus) bacteria under visible light treatment. Table 2 shows results of antimicrobial activities of CQD synthesized from SCG under visible light irradiation depending on different pH conditions against E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in PBS. Initial bacterial concentrations of E. coli O157:H7 in PBS with 1 mg/mL of CQD at pH 2.5, 3.0, and 4.0 were 7.36, 7.46, and 7.43 log CFU/mL, respectively. Initial concentrations of E. coli O157:H7 in PBS without CQD at pH 2.5, 3.0, and 4.0 were 7.20, 7.71, and 7.30 log CFU/mL, respectively. Initial bacterial concentrations of S. aureus in PBS with 1 mg/mL of CQD at pH 2.5, 3.0, and 4.0 were 7.56, 7.48, and 7.53 log CFU/mL, respectively. Initial concentrations of E. coli O157:H7 in PBS without CQD at pH 2.5, 3.0, and 4.0 were 7.68, 7.49, and 7.49 log CFU/mL, respectively. Regardless of the presence or absence of CQD, significant (P < 0.05) reduction of E. coli O157:H7 was observed from 14.04 J/cm2 (30 min treatment) and significant (P < 0.05) reduction of S. aureus was observed from 84.24 J/cm2 (3 h treatment) in the dark state at pH 2.5. There was no significant difference (P > 0.05) in the reduction of either pathogen between treatment with CQD and those without CQD. At pH 3.0 and 4.0, there was no significant (P > 0.05) reduction for either pathogen during 84.24 J/cm2 treatment in the dark state. Since the acidic condition affected reduction results, values were processed by subtracting the reduction value after light treatment by reduction value in the dark state. This was expressed as Light – Dark to compare the VLD antimicrobial effect. The reduction value of Light – Dark in the presence of CQD (1 mg/mL) was significantly (P < 0.05) greater than that in the absence of CQD, meaning that CQD derived from SCG exhibited VLD antimicrobial activity. Although several studies have reported that CQD exhibits an antimicrobial activity through a catalytic reaction under visible light (Al Awak et al., 2017; Dong et al., 2018; Kang and Kang, 2021; Meziani et al., 2016; Rabe et al., 2019), the present study is the first to verify that CQD synthesized using biomass exhibits VLD antimicrobial activity. Moreover, reduction values of Light – Dark showed that as the pH decreased, the VLD antimicrobial activity of CQD increased. In particular, at pH 4, the VLD antimicrobial activity of CQD did not appear after treatment with 84.24 J/cm2 of visible light. The same trend was observed under neutral conditions (pH 7.0) and alkaline conditions (pH 11.0) (data not shown). This means that VDL antimicrobial activity of CQD synthesized from SCG is more effective when it is utilized under low pH conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in photodynamic properties of CQD synthesized from SCG at different pH conditions
Effect of zeta potential (mV) change of CQD and pathogens according to pH condition on uptake of CQD into cells
Changes in activities of ROS defense enzymes within cells at different pH conditions
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in previous results, the fact that changes in physical properties, such as surface charge of CQDs can be induced by pH conditions means that pH conditions can also change properties of bacterial pathogens, which might have influenced the change in sensitivity to CQDs. The VLD antimicrobial activity of CQD is mainly due to ROS it generates (Kang and Kang, 2021). Living organisms can generate ROS as by-products of aerobic metabolism. They can protect themselves aginst oxidative damage caused by ROS using several defense systems that can scavenge ROS (Yao et al., 2006). Catalase (CAT) and superoxide dismutase (SOD) are the most well-known enzymes in the ROS defense system of cells (Li et al., 2007). As extracellular pH changes, intracellular pH also changes within a positive correlation due to pH gradient (Campolongo et al., 2014; Hansen et al., 2016). It is known that activities of ROS defense enzymes will decreases when pH decreases from a neutral pH (Alptekin et al., 2008; Danial and Alkhalaf, 2020). Based on this fact, we hypothesized that activities of ROS defense enzymes would change with changing internal pH of pathogens when pH conditions were changed. Thus, we measured activities of CAT and SOD as representative ROS defense enzymes under each pH condition (pH 2.5, 3.0, and 4.0). As expected, activities of ROS defense enzymes (SOD and CAT) of both E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus decreased significantly (P < 0.05) as the pH decreased (Fig. 8). Their activities might have gradually decreased due to changes in three-dimensional structures of enzymes according to a decrease of pH inside cells (Scopes, 2001). It has been reported that sodA sodB mutant E. coli shows increased sensitivity to UVA, a ROS-related microbial control technology, than the wild type (Hoerter et al., 2005). It has also been reported that the levels of SOD and CAT in Photobacterium phosphoreum are higher than those in Alteromonas alvinellae, leading to resistance of P. phosphoreum than A. alvinellae to photocatalytic oxidation (Leung et al., 2008). This means that ROS defense enzymes such as SOD and CAT play an important role in the resistance of microorganisms to ROS-involved control technologies. In fact, our previous studies have also demonstrated that when the activity of ROS defense enzyme of pathogenic bacteria is reduced by organic acid or mild heating, the antimicrobial activity of plasma activated water or KrCl excimer lamp is increaseed (Jyung et al., 2023; Kang et al., 2020a,b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in the degree of intracellular ROS (iROS) generation and oxidative damage at different pH conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Combining results of Figs. 6–8 , it could be interpreted that as pH decreases, the electrostatic repulsion between CQD and pathogen also decreases, resulting in enhanced penetration of CQD into pathogens. At the same time, the ROS scavenging activity decreases. Thus, the amount of ROS remaining without being scavenged increases in addition to the occurrence of more ROS inside cells, resulting in increased cell damage caused by ROS, which can eventually leads to an increase in VLD antimicrobial activity of CQD. To prove this interpretation, we first determined the amount of ROS generated inside cells caused by CQD at each pH condition (pH 2.5, 3.0, and 4.0) under visible light treatment. In this case, S. aureus is suitable for confirming the effect of VLD antimicrobial activity because it is highly resistant to acidic condition, thus excluding the effect of pH. Thus, S. aureus was representatively examined. To measure the amount of ROS accumulated in cells, iROS generation values were measured using H2DCFDA that could permeate into cells and become oxidized by iROS to 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) known to emit fluorescence. Thus, the amount of iROS generation can be measured based on the degree of DCF generation (Yoon et al., 2018). After treatment with 84.24 J/cm2 of visible light, iROS was not generated at pH 4.0 regardless of the presence or absence of CQD. However, iROS was generated at pH 3.0 and 2.5 in the presence of CQD, showing significantly (P < 0.05) higher values at pH 2.5 (Fig. 9 ). To examine whether the increased amount of iROS actually induced more cellular damage, damage to cell membrane that could lead to death by reducing the ability of microorganisms to maintain homeostasis for life activities was measured representatively among various cell components (Park and Kang, 2013). The degree of cell membrane damage was measured using PI that could not penetrate an intact membrane, but could emit fluorescence by penetrating the cell membrane with physical destruction (Hewitt & Nebe-Von-Caron, 2004). As shown in Fig. 10 A, when 84.24 J/cm2 of visible light was used for treatment in the presence of CQD, cell membrane damage did not appear at pH 4.0, whereas damage was observed at pH 2.5 and 3.0, showing significantly (P < 0.05) larger value at pH 2.5, consistent with results of iROS generation and antimicrobial effect. Finally, to prove a direct correlation between the amount of iROS generation and cell membrane damage, it was necessary to confirm that the cell membrane damage was caused by ROS. Fatty acid, one of the essential cellular materials, is mainly present in the cell membrane in the form of acyl phospholipid. More peroxidation of lipids in the cell membrane will lead to more permeability of cells, which may cause cell membrane damage. (Gutteridge, 1995; Kaneda, 1991). Therefore, the degree of lipid peroxidation in the cell membrane was measured using DPPP. DPPP could react with lipid hydroperoxide in the cell membrane and oxidize in the form of DPPP=O which emits fluorescence (Okimoto et al., 2000). As shown in Fig. 10B, the occurrence of lipid peroxidation in the cell membrane showed a trend consistent with the result of cell membrane damage. In conclusion, a series of experimental results proved that as pH decreased, the uptake of CQDs into bacterial pathogens increased and the ROS defense enzyme activity decreased, directly leading to an increase in the occurrence of oxidative damage to microorganisms by CQD. In order to provide a clear understanding of this mechanistic interpretation, this has been rearranged and summarized in the form of graphical abstract to provide additional explanation (see Fig. 11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of CQD to fresh produce washing
ce:section.ce:para: Sanitizers, including sodium hypochlorite, hydrogen peroxide, organic acids, and peroxyacetic acid, are extensively employed in the disinfection of fruits and vegetables due to their economical nature and remarkable disinfecting capabilities (Sethi et al., 2020). Among them, organic acids are naturally occurring compounds present in a variety of fruits and fermented items and have obtained the designation of generally recognized as safe (GRAS) antimicrobial agents from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) (Beier, 2021). Organic acids offer distinct advantages when compared to sodium hypochlorite, primarily stemming from their favorable toxicological safety profile and their environmentally friendly attributes (Turhan et al., 2022). However, employing elevated concentrations of organic acids, which can effectively combat pathogens on food surface, can lead to undesirable alterations in food products, such as skin discoloration, and pose increased costs for food processors and in contrast, utilizing lower concentrations of organic acids has demonstrated limited effectiveness (Zaki et al., 2015). Therefore, this means that a strategy is needed to reduce the treatment concentration or time of organic acids and improve control effects on pathogenic bacteria.

In this context, based on previous results showing that CQDs synthesized from SCG have higher VLD antimicrobial activity at lower pH, we judged that combining organic acids with CQDs could exhibit a synergistic effect by complementing each other's limitations, making them useful for practical application in the food industry. To verify this, the washing effect was observed under visible light treatment of a combination of CQD and malic acid synthesized from SCG on samples prepared by artificially contaminating the surface of apple, a model food, with E. coli O157:H7, a major pathogenic baterium in fresh produce (Fig. 12 ).

When washed with 1 mg/ml CQD solution without malic acid, after 30 min of treatment without or with visible light irradiation (=14.04 J/cm2), cell level of E. coli O157:H7 on the apple surface decreased from 8.72 log to 7.56 log and 7.59 log, respectively, showing no significant (P > 0.05) difference in reduction values of between them. This means that the VLD antimicrobial effect of CQD without malic acid does not appear within the visible light irradiation range of 14.04 J/cm2. It only shows a washing out effect. The level of E. coli O157:H7 in DW, the washing solution, was also 7.90 log after treatment for 30 min without or with visible light irradiation (=14.04 J/cm2), meaning that E. coli O157:H7 washed out from the sample surface remained in washing solution at a high concentration. On the other hand, when malic acid was combined with CQD solution, the VLD antimicrobial effect against E. coli O157:H7 was not only shown on the apple surface, but also in the washing solution. Its effect was significantly (P < 0.05) increased as the concentration of malic acid increased. Although the control effect could be due to the antimicrobial effect of malic acid itself, the control effect was significantly increased due to VLD antimicrobial activity of CQD when the sample was treated with visible light. In addition, when examining the change in food quality in response to treatment in 2.0% malic acid CQD solution, there was no significant (P > 0.05) change in the color or texture of apple surface until 14.04 J/cm2 treatment (data not shown). In other words, this result means that applying CQD synthesized from SCG to organic acid washing can effectively reduce the concentration and treatment time required for washing processing, reducing cost and contributing greatly to ensuring food safety by inducing excellent microbial control effects. Although there have been various combination attempts to improve control effects of these organic acids on pathogenic bacteria in fresh produce, such as using ultrasound (Turhan et al., 2022), residual chlorine (Chhetri et al., 2019), mild heat (L. Chen et al., 2022), xenon light (Cho and Ha, 2021), and plasma-activated water (Jyung et al., 2023), this results are significant and meaningful in that this study provides novel and practical basic data for utilizing CQDs synthesized from food by-products, which have not been reported before.

ce:list-item.ce:para: CQDs were easily and successfully synthesized from SCG using microwave heating.
CQDs showed increased VLD antimicrobial activity as pH decreased.

Mechanism analysis revealed that as pH decreased, physical interaction between CQDs and pathogens increased and the activity of ROS defense enzyme of pathogens decreased, thereby improving the VLD antimicrobial activity of CQDs.

It was confirmed that the technology combining CQD and organic acid synthesized from SCG could be applied to fresh produce washing process to control pathogenic bacteria on fresh produce surfaces and in the washing solution.
This is the first study that suggests the possibility of using CQDs synthesized using food by-products as a food microorganism control technology in the food industry.
